threats.name: SQL Injection
threats.description: Attacker injects malicious SQL queries through user input to manipulate database operations

controls.description: Implement parameterized queries or prepared statements
Apply strict input validation and sanitization
Use ORM frameworks that automatically escape inputs
Implement least-privilege database accounts
Enable database activity monitoring and alerting

threats.name: NoSQL Injection
threats.description: Attacker manipulates NoSQL queries through unsanitized input to access or modify data

controls.description: Validate and sanitize all user inputs
Use parameterized queries where supported
Implement schema validation
Apply principle of least privilege for database access

threats.name: Unauthorized Access
threats.description: Attacker gains access to resources without proper authentication or authorization

controls.description: Implement strong authentication mechanisms (MFA)
Enforce least-privilege access controls
Implement network segmentation and firewalls
Enable comprehensive access logging and monitoring
Regularly review and audit access permissions

threats.name: Security Misconfiguration
threats.description: Insecure default configurations or incomplete setup creates security vulnerabilities

controls.description: Implement infrastructure as code with security baselines
Perform regular security configuration audits
Use automated configuration scanning tools
Disable unnecessary services and features
Follow cloud provider security best practices

threats.name: Data Exfiltration
threats.description: Unauthorized extraction of sensitive data from the organization

controls.description: Implement data loss prevention (DLP) tools
Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
Monitor and alert on unusual data transfers
Implement egress filtering and monitoring
Classify and label sensitive data

threats.name: Privilege Escalation
threats.description: Attacker elevates their access rights to gain higher-level permissions

controls.description: Implement principle of least privilege
Regularly patch and update systems
Monitor for suspicious privilege changes
Use privileged access management (PAM) solutions
Disable unnecessary SUID/SGID binaries

threats.name: Denial of Service
threats.description: Attack aimed at making services unavailable to legitimate users

controls.description: Implement rate limiting and throttling
Use DDoS protection services
Configure auto-scaling for critical services
Implement CDN for traffic distribution
Set up monitoring and alerting for traffic anomalies

threats.name: Malware Infection
threats.description: Malicious software installed on systems to perform unauthorized actions

controls.description: Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions
Implement application whitelisting
Keep systems patched and updated
Disable unnecessary scripting engines
Implement email and web filtering

threats.name: Code Injection
threats.description: Attacker injects malicious code that gets executed by the application

controls.description: Validate and sanitize all user inputs
Use parameterized queries and prepared statements
Implement Content Security Policy (CSP)
Apply output encoding for different contexts
Use Web Application Firewalls (WAF)

threats.name: Excessive Permissions
threats.description: Services or users granted more permissions than necessary for their function

controls.description: Implement principle of least privilege
Conduct regular permission audits
Use role-based access control (RBAC)
Implement just-in-time (JIT) access
Automate permission rightsizing

threats.name: Sensitive Data Exposure
threats.description: Sensitive data exposed through improper storage, transmission, or access controls

controls.description: Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
Implement proper access controls
Use secrets management solutions
Mask sensitive data in logs and outputs
Implement data classification and handling policies

threats.name: Storage Bucket Misconfiguration
threats.description: Cloud storage buckets configured with overly permissive access policies

controls.description: Block public access by default
Implement bucket policies with least privilege
Enable access logging and monitoring
Use automated scanning for public buckets
Encrypt all stored objects

threats.name: Container Escape
threats.description: Attacker breaks out of container isolation to access the host system

controls.description: Run containers as non-root users
Use read-only file systems where possible
Implement pod security policies/standards
Disable privileged containers
Use container runtime security tools

threats.name: Supply Chain Attack
threats.description: Malicious code introduced through compromised dependencies or build processes

controls.description: Scan container images for vulnerabilities
Use signed and verified images only
Implement software bill of materials (SBOM)
Use private container registries
Pin dependency versions and verify checksums

threats.name: Credential Theft
threats.description: Attackers steal credentials to gain unauthorized access to systems

controls.description: Implement multi-factor authentication
Rotate credentials regularly
Use secrets management solutions
Monitor for credential misuse
Implement credential-less authentication where possible

threats.name: Audit Logging Bypass
threats.description: Attacker disables or evades security logging to hide malicious activity

controls.description: Implement immutable logging
Ship logs to secure, separate storage
Monitor for logging gaps or anomalies
Implement log integrity verification
Alert on logging configuration changes
Monitor for security service status changes (e.g., detector disabled, logging stopped)

threats.name: Network Misconfiguration
threats.description: Improper network security group or firewall rules expose resources

controls.description: Implement default-deny firewall rules
Regularly audit security group configurations
Use network segmentation
Implement network flow logging
Use infrastructure as code for network config

threats.name: Lateral Movement
threats.description: Attacker moves between systems within the network after initial compromise

controls.description: Implement network segmentation and micro-segmentation
Use zero-trust network architecture
Monitor for unusual internal traffic patterns
Implement jump servers for administrative access
Disable unnecessary remote access protocols

threats.name: Broken Authentication
threats.description: Weak authentication mechanisms allow attackers to compromise user accounts

controls.description: Implement multi-factor authentication
Enforce strong password policies
Implement account lockout mechanisms
Use secure session management
Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints

threats.name: Message Tampering
threats.description: Attacker modifies messages in transit between services

controls.description: Encrypt messages in transit using TLS
Implement message signing and verification
Use authenticated encryption
Validate message integrity on receipt
Implement dead-letter queue monitoring and alerting for tampered or poisoned messages
Restrict producer access using IAM policies or broker-level authorization

threats.name: Log Tampering
threats.description: Attacker modifies or deletes logs to hide malicious activity

controls.description: Implement write-once log storage
Ship logs to secure centralized storage
Implement log integrity monitoring
Restrict log deletion permissions
Enable object lock or legal hold on log storage for compliance retention periods

threats.name: Backup Data Exposure
threats.description: Database backups stored insecurely expose sensitive data

controls.description: Encrypt all backup data at rest
Implement strict access controls on backups
Store backups in separate security zone
Regularly test backup restoration procedures

threats.name: Unpatched Vulnerabilities
threats.description: Known security vulnerabilities remain unpatched, enabling exploitation

controls.description: Implement automated patch management
Perform regular vulnerability scanning
Establish patch SLAs based on severity
Use vulnerability management platform
Implement compensating controls for delayed patches

threats.name: Man-in-the-Middle Attack
threats.description: Attacker intercepts and potentially modifies communication between two connected services

controls.description: Encrypt all traffic using TLS 1.2 or higher
Implement mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication
Use certificate pinning where applicable
Implement network segmentation

threats.name: Data Exposure in Transit
threats.description: Sensitive data transmitted between services may be exposed if not properly encrypted

controls.description: Encrypt all data in transit using TLS
Avoid transmitting sensitive data when possible
Implement end-to-end encryption for highly sensitive data
Use secure protocols (HTTPS, SFTP, etc.)

threats.name: Replay Attack
threats.description: Attacker captures and retransmits valid data transmissions to perform unauthorized actions

controls.description: Implement request timestamps and nonces
Use short-lived tokens and session identifiers
Implement message sequence numbers
Use TLS to prevent packet capture

threats.name: Protocol Injection
threats.description: Attacker injects malicious data into the communication channel between services

controls.description: Validate and sanitize all incoming data
Use message authentication codes (MACs)
Implement strict protocol parsing
Use encrypted and authenticated protocols

threats.name: Connection Flooding
threats.description: Attacker overwhelms the connection between services to disrupt communication

controls.description: Implement rate limiting between services
Use connection pooling and timeouts
Deploy load balancers with DDoS protection
Implement circuit breakers for resilience

threats.name: Prompt Injection
threats.description: Attacker manipulates AI model inputs to bypass safety controls, extract sensitive information, or cause unintended behavior

controls.description: Implement input validation and sanitization for all prompts
Use system prompts that establish clear behavioral boundaries
Implement output filtering to prevent sensitive data leakage
Monitor and log AI interactions for anomalous patterns
Apply rate limiting per user and session
Implement platform-specific content safety guardrails (e.g., Bedrock Guardrails, Azure Content Safety, Vertex AI Safety Settings)
Isolate user-provided input from system instructions using structured API formats

threats.name: Training Data Poisoning
threats.description: Attacker manipulates training data or fine-tuning datasets to introduce backdoors or biases into AI models

controls.description: Validate and sanitize all training data sources
Implement data provenance tracking
Use anomaly detection on training datasets
Conduct regular model behavior audits
Implement access controls and audit logging on training data storage
Verify training data integrity using checksums or cryptographic hashes before training runs

threats.name: Model Extraction
threats.description: Attacker queries AI model systematically to reconstruct or steal proprietary model weights and architecture

controls.description: Implement strict rate limiting on model API endpoints
Monitor for systematic querying patterns
Add output perturbation to prevent exact replication
Restrict API access with strong authentication

threats.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
threats.description: Attacker tricks server into making requests to internal resources or external systems

controls.description: Validate and sanitize all user-supplied URLs
Implement allowlists for permitted destination hosts
Block requests to internal IP ranges and metadata endpoints
Use network segmentation to limit server access
Disable unnecessary URL schemes (file://, gopher://, etc.)

threats.name: Cache Poisoning
threats.description: Attacker injects malicious content into cache to serve compromised data to users

controls.description: Implement strict cache key validation
Use signed URLs or cache tokens
Configure appropriate cache headers and TTLs
Monitor cache hit rates for anomalies

threats.name: Event Injection
threats.description: Attacker injects malicious events into event-driven systems to trigger unauthorized actions

controls.description: Validate event schema and content before processing
Implement event signing and verification
Use IAM policies to restrict event sources
Monitor event patterns for anomalies

threats.name: Data Stream Hijacking
threats.description: Attacker intercepts or manipulates real-time data streams to inject false data or steal information

controls.description: Encrypt data streams in transit using TLS
Implement producer authentication and authorization
Use message signing for integrity verification
Monitor stream throughput and patterns for anomalies
Secure checkpoint and offset storage to prevent consumer position manipulation
Implement consumer group isolation to prevent unauthorized consumers from joining

threats.name: Workflow Manipulation
threats.description: Attacker exploits workflow orchestration to bypass security controls or execute unauthorized steps

controls.description: Implement least-privilege execution roles for each workflow step
Validate state transitions and inputs at each step
Enable comprehensive workflow execution logging
Use workflow versioning and change management

threats.name: Authentication Token Theft
threats.description: Attacker steals authentication tokens to impersonate users or gain unauthorized access

controls.description: Use short-lived tokens with automatic refresh
Implement token binding to client attributes
Store tokens securely (HttpOnly, Secure cookies)
Implement token revocation mechanisms
Monitor for token reuse from multiple locations

threats.name: Account Takeover
threats.description: Attacker gains control of user accounts through credential stuffing, phishing, or session hijacking

controls.description: Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA)
Implement adaptive authentication based on risk signals
Monitor for suspicious login patterns
Implement account lockout and notification policies
Check credentials against known breach databases

threats.name: OAuth Token Abuse
threats.description: Attackers exploit OAuth tokens to access connected applications, exfiltrate data, or perform unauthorized actions across integrated services

controls.description: Implement least-privilege OAuth scopes for all integrations
Regularly audit and revoke unused OAuth app authorizations
Monitor OAuth token usage for anomalous patterns
Implement OAuth app allowlisting policies
Use short-lived tokens with automatic refresh

threats.name: Third-Party Integration Risk
threats.description: Insecure integrations with third-party applications create attack vectors or expose sensitive data through connected services

controls.description: Maintain inventory of all third-party integrations and their permissions
Implement security review process for new integrations
Monitor data flows between integrated applications
Enforce API rate limiting and access controls
Regularly review and remove unused integrations

threats.name: SaaS Data Leakage
threats.description: Sensitive data unintentionally shared externally through SaaS collaboration features such as file sharing, public links, or guest access

controls.description: Configure external sharing policies and restrictions
Implement DLP policies for sensitive content detection
Monitor and audit external sharing activities
Disable public link sharing for sensitive content
Implement data classification and labeling

threats.name: Shadow IT
threats.description: Unauthorized SaaS applications used by employees bypass security controls, create visibility gaps, and may expose corporate data

controls.description: Deploy CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) for SaaS visibility
Implement network monitoring for unauthorized SaaS usage
Establish approved SaaS application catalog
Provide easy request process for new SaaS applications
Conduct regular shadow IT discovery and remediation

threats.name: Data Classification Evasion
threats.description: Attacker structures, encodes, or obfuscates sensitive data to evade automated detection and classification systems

controls.description: Implement multiple detection methods (pattern, ML, context-aware)
Monitor for encoding and obfuscation patterns
Apply classification at multiple points in data lifecycle
Regularly update detection rules and patterns
Implement human review for edge cases and anomalies

threats.name: ETL Pipeline Manipulation
threats.description: Attacker modifies data transformation logic or job definitions to corrupt data, exfiltrate information, or inject malicious content

controls.description: Implement strict access controls on pipeline definitions
Use version control and code review for transformation logic
Monitor pipeline modifications and execution patterns
Implement data validation at pipeline stages
Use separate credentials for pipeline execution with least privilege

threats.name: Unauthorized Remote Command Execution
threats.description: Attacker leverages management tools to execute unauthorized commands on target systems

controls.description: Implement strict IAM policies for command execution
Enable comprehensive logging of all remote commands
Use session recording for administrative access
Implement approval workflows for sensitive operations
Restrict command execution to approved runbooks

threats.name: Batch Job Tampering
threats.description: Attacker modifies batch job definitions, scripts, or execution environment to execute malicious code or access unauthorized resources

controls.description: Implement strict access controls on job definitions
Use signed and verified container images for jobs
Monitor job execution for anomalous behavior
Implement network isolation for batch compute environments
Use separate IAM roles with least privilege for each job type

threats.name: GraphQL API Abuse
threats.description: Attacker exploits GraphQL-specific vulnerabilities including query depth attacks, introspection abuse, batching attacks, or field-level authorization bypass

controls.description: Implement query depth and complexity limits
Disable introspection in production environments
Implement field-level authorization checks
Use query cost analysis and rate limiting
Validate and sanitize all query inputs

threats.name: Search Index Poisoning
threats.description: Attacker manipulates search indexes to inject malicious content, alter search rankings, or expose sensitive data through search results

controls.description: Implement strict access controls on index management
Validate and sanitize all content before indexing
Monitor index modifications for anomalies
Implement search result filtering based on user permissions
Use separate indexes for different sensitivity levels

threats.name: Hybrid Infrastructure Attack
threats.description: Attacker exploits hybrid cloud connections to pivot between on-premises and cloud environments or intercept traffic on dedicated connections

controls.description: Implement strong authentication for hybrid connections
Encrypt all traffic over dedicated connections
Monitor hybrid connection traffic for anomalies
Implement network segmentation between environments
Use private endpoints and avoid public internet routing

threats.name: Data Lake Governance Bypass
threats.description: Attacker circumvents data lake access controls, column/row-level security, or data masking policies to access restricted data

controls.description: Implement centralized access control policies
Enable comprehensive audit logging for all data access
Use column and row-level security for sensitive data
Implement data masking for sensitive fields
Regularly audit and review data access patterns

threats.name: Kubernetes Secrets Exposure
threats.description: Attacker accesses Kubernetes secrets stored in etcd, which may contain credentials, API keys, TLS certificates, and other sensitive data often stored without encryption-at-rest by default

controls.description: Enable etcd encryption-at-rest using EncryptionConfiguration
Use external secrets management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) instead of native Kubernetes secrets
Implement strict RBAC policies limiting access to secrets
Enable audit logging for all secrets access
Use sealed-secrets or external-secrets operator for GitOps workflows

threats.name: Cluster State Manipulation
threats.description: Attacker directly modifies cluster state in etcd, bypassing Kubernetes API server admission controllers and RBAC to create privileged workloads, modify RBAC rules, or corrupt cluster configuration

controls.description: Restrict direct etcd access to only the Kubernetes API server
Require mutual TLS (mTLS) for all etcd client connections
Run etcd on dedicated nodes with no other workloads
Enable etcd audit logging and monitor for direct API access
Implement network policies to isolate etcd from pod network

threats.name: etcd Snapshot Exposure
threats.description: Attacker accesses etcd snapshots or backups which contain complete cluster state including all secrets, ConfigMaps, service account tokens, and RBAC configurations

controls.description: Encrypt etcd snapshots at rest using strong encryption
Store snapshots in access-controlled storage with audit logging
Implement backup retention policies and secure deletion
Use separate credentials for backup operations with minimal permissions
Monitor access to snapshot storage locations

threats.name: Distributed Consensus Disruption
threats.description: Attacker disrupts the Raft consensus protocol by manipulating leader election, injecting rogue members, or causing network partitions that lead to split-brain scenarios or data inconsistency

controls.description: Require peer certificate authentication for all etcd members
Use static member configuration rather than dynamic discovery
Deploy etcd across multiple availability zones with proper quorum
Monitor cluster health metrics and member status
Implement network segmentation for etcd peer traffic

threats.name: Phishing
threats.description: Adversary uses deceptive emails, messages, voice calls, or look-alike sites to trick users into revealing credentials, executing malicious payloads, or approving fraudulent transactions. Targeted variants (spearphishing, business email compromise) impersonate specific colleagues or vendors and are a primary initial-access vector for cloud account takeover.

controls.description: Enforce phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) for all user accounts
Deploy a secure email gateway with link rewriting, attachment sandboxing, and impersonation detection
Run periodic phishing simulations and provide an in-client report-phish button
Apply conditional access policies that block legacy authentication and require compliant devices
Tag external-sender emails with a visible warning banner
Require out-of-band verification for sensitive financial or credential-change requests

threats.name: Insider Threat
threats.description: Authorised individuals (employees, contractors, or partners) misuse legitimate access to exfiltrate data, sabotage systems, or commit fraud — either intentionally or through negligence. Distinct from credential theft because the actor already holds valid permissions, which makes detection dependent on behavioural rather than authentication signals.

controls.description: Enforce least-privilege and just-in-time access with mandatory approval workflows for sensitive actions
Deploy User and Entity Behaviour Analytics (UEBA) to detect anomalous access patterns
Apply separation of duties for high-risk operations (production data access, financial transactions, key management)
Run a joiner-mover-leaver process that promptly revokes access on role change or departure
Use tamper-evident audit logging shipped to a system the actor cannot modify
Apply DLP on email, file sharing, and removable media to detect bulk data egress

threats.name: Ransomware
threats.description: Adversary encrypts or destroys organisational data and demands payment for decryption. Modern variants combine encryption with double-extortion exfiltration, threatening to publish stolen data even if backups allow recovery. Cloud-targeted ransomware additionally abuses identity to delete snapshots, rotate KMS keys, or wipe object storage.

controls.description: Maintain immutable, offline, and regularly tested backups following the 3-2-1 rule
Use object-lock or write-once storage and protect snapshot deletion with separate credentials and MFA
Network-segment to limit lateral movement; disable SMB/RDP exposure to the internet
Deploy EDR with anti-ransomware behavioural detection and automated isolation
Enforce phishing-resistant MFA and disable legacy authentication on identity providers
Apply application allowlisting and restrict macro and script execution on endpoints
Maintain and rehearse an incident response plan including ransom-payment policy and recovery runbooks

threats.name: DNS Hijacking
threats.description: Adversary takes control of DNS resolution to redirect traffic to attacker-controlled infrastructure for credential capture, traffic interception, or malware delivery. Attack surfaces include compromised registrar accounts, unauthorised changes to authoritative records, resolver cache poisoning, and DNS rebinding attacks that abuse browser same-origin assumptions to reach internal services.

controls.description: Enable DNSSEC on authoritative zones and validate responses on resolvers
Apply registrar lock and require MFA on domain-registrar and DNS-provider accounts
Alert on unauthorised DNS record changes and monitor certificate transparency logs for unexpected issuance
Mitigate DNS rebinding by validating Host headers server-side and rejecting RFC1918 answers from external resolvers
Use private DNS zones for internal services and avoid resolving them via public resolvers
Pin clients to trusted resolvers using DoH/DoT where feasible

threats.name: Email Spoofing
threats.description: Adversary forges email sender identity to impersonate trusted parties for phishing, fraud, or business email compromise. Enabled by missing or permissive SPF/DKIM/DMARC records, open mail relays, or look-alike domains; closely paired with phishing as the delivery technique but distinct in that the failure is in mail-authentication policy rather than user judgement.

controls.description: Publish a strict DMARC policy (p=reject) with aligned SPF and DKIM for every sending domain, including parked ones
Configure inbound mail servers to enforce DMARC and reject or quarantine failures
Deploy MTA-STS and TLS-RPT to enforce authenticated transport between mail servers
Monitor DMARC aggregate and forensic reports to detect abuse and unauthorised senders
Apply external-sender warning banners and detect display-name impersonation of executives
Defensively register and lock common typo-squat and homoglyph variants of corporate domains
